• Singer acknowledges this conclusion but argues that most people give far less than they could without significant sacrifice.
• Philosopher Susan Wolf argues that Singer’s view leaves no room for the personal projects and relationships that give life meaning.
The student wants to present the strongest objection to Singer’s argument. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices: Peter Singer argues that wealthy individuals are morally obligated to donate significantly to effective charities in order to reduce global poverty.
Susan Wolf argues that Singer’s view is problematic because it leaves no room for the personal projects and relationships that make a meaningful life possible.
Singer acknowledges that his principle could require giving until it harms the giver, but argues that most people currently give far less than they could without significant sacrifice.
Critics note that Singer’s principle, taken to its logical conclusion, would require individuals to give until further giving would harm themselves as much as it helps others.
questions.explanation:
For Rhetorical Synthesis questions, the goal determines the correct answer. The student wants to present the strongest objection to Singer’s argument — the answer must challenge Singer’s framework most fundamentally, not just note a complication.
A) This describes Singer’s own position, not an objection to it. Eliminate A.
B) Wolf’s objection challenges the foundation of Singer’s framework — if his view makes a meaningful life impossible, the entire ethical system is called into question. This is the most fundamental challenge. Keep B.
C) This presents Singer’s own response to a criticism, not the objection itself. Presenting his rebuttal does not accomplish the goal of stating an objection. Eliminate C.
D) This is also a strong objection, but it focuses on the logical extreme of Singer’s principle rather than challenging the framework itself. Wolf’s objection is broader and more fundamental — it questions what kind of life Singer’s view permits, not just how far the principle extends. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

questions.subject: verbal

stemSegments.text: In his 1855 poem “Song of Myself,” Walt Whitman presents the self as expansive and fundamentally contradictory — capable of containing opposing ideas and impulses without the need for internal consistency, writing _______.
Which quotation from “Song of Myself” most effectively illustrates the claim?

choices: “I celebrate myself, and sing myself, / And what I assume you shall assume, / For every atom belonging to me as good belongs to you.”
“Do I contradict myself? / Very well then I contradict myself, / (I am large, I contain multitudes.)”
“I am the poet of the Body and I am the poet of the Soul.”
“I have said that the soul is not more than the body, / And I have said that the body is not more than the soul.”
questions.explanation:
For Illustrating Claims with Quotations questions, identify the precise claim before evaluating the choices. The claim has two parts: (1) the self is expansive and contradictory, and (2) it contains opposing ideas without needing internal consistency. The correct quotation must illustrate both.
A) This opening stanza emphasizes unity between the self and others (“every atom belonging to me as good belongs to you”) — it is about connection, not internal contradiction. Eliminate A.
B) Directly addresses self-contradiction (“Do I contradict myself? Very well then I contradict myself”) and the expansive self (“I am large, I contain multitudes”) — both parts of the claim are illustrated. Keep B.
C) Presents a duality (body and soul) but frames it as two roles the speaker occupies, not as a contradiction the self embraces without concern for consistency. Eliminate C.
D) Asserts the equal value of body and soul but does not address contradiction or the self’s capacity to contain opposing impulses without resolution. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.stem:
When Ignaz Semmelweis proposed in 1847 that physicians were transmitting fatal infections to patients by failing to wash their hands between performing autopsies and delivering babies, his colleagues largely rejected the theory. The medical establishment found the idea that doctors themselves could be causing patient deaths implausible and offensive to professional dignity. Semmelweis grew increasingly frustrated and erratic in his advocacy, ultimately being committed to a mental institution where he died in 1865 — ironically, likely from the same type of infection he had spent his career trying to prevent. His theory was vindicated only after Louis Pasteur’s germ theory provided a scientific mechanism to explain it.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?
choices: Semmelweis failed to prevent hospital infections because he lacked access to modern sanitation equipment.
Despite proposing a correct and potentially life-saving theory, Semmelweis was rejected by the medical establishment and died without seeing his work validated.
The medical community’s resistance to Semmelweis’s theory demonstrates that scientific progress is always contentious and slow.
Pasteur’s germ theory was more scientifically rigorous than Semmelweis’s handwashing hypothesis and ultimately replaced it.
questions.explanation:
For Main Idea questions at level 3, the correct answer must capture the full arc of the passage precisely — not just one element. Before looking at choices, summarize: Semmelweis had a correct theory, was rejected for non-scientific reasons, and died before his work was accepted.
A) The passage does not mention sanitation equipment — Semmelweis’s proposal was specifically about handwashing, not equipment. This distorts the content. Eliminate A.
B) Captures all three key elements: his theory was correct, the establishment rejected it, and he died without vindication. Keep B.
C) Overgeneralizes from one case to a broad claim about all scientific progress. The passage does not make this argument — it tells one specific story. Eliminate C.
D) Misrepresents the relationship between Semmelweis and Pasteur. Pasteur’s theory explained and vindicated Semmelweis’s work — it did not replace or supersede it. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

questions.subject: verbal

stemSegments.text: Average Commute Time and Remote Work Rate by City

CityAvg Commute Time (min)% Remote Workers
City A4812%
City B5128%
City C528%
City D2941%
City E616%

A researcher argues that among these five cities, higher rates of remote work are consistently associated with shorter average commute times, and that this relationship holds across all five cities in the data. Which choice most accurately uses data from the table to evaluate the researcher's claim?
Which choice most accurately uses data from the table to evaluate the researcher's claim?

choices: City D has the highest remote work rate and the shortest commute time, which supports the researcher's claim.

The data largely support the researcher's claim, but City B, which has a higher remote work rate than City A, also has a longer commute time, contradicting the claim that the relationship holds across all five cities.
City E has both the longest commute time and the lowest remote work rate, which is consistent with the researcher's claim.
Cities with remote work rates above 25% all have commute times below 40 minutes, fully supporting the researcher's claim.
questions.explanation:
For Quantitative Evidence questions, verify each claim carefully against the table before selecting an answer. The researcher claims the relationship holds across all five cities. Check each pair: City E (6%, 61 min), City C (8%, 52 min), City A (12%, 48 min) — all follow the pattern. But City B (28% remote) has a longer commute (51 min) than City A (12% remote, 48 min) — the pattern breaks.
A) True but incomplete — City D supporting the claim at one end of the range does not confirm the claim holds across all five cities. Eliminate A.
B) Correctly identifies that the pattern mostly holds but notes the exception: City B has more remote workers than City A but a longer commute, directly contradicting the claim that the relationship holds across all five cities. Keep B.
C) True but addresses only one data point. Showing one city fits the pattern does not evaluate whether the pattern holds for all five. Eliminate C.
D) Factually incorrect — City B has a 28% remote work rate but a 51-minute commute, well above 40 minutes. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

questions.subject: verbal

stemSegments.text: Adults Reporting High Stress Levels by Occupation (%)

Occupation20192022
Healthcare workers41%68%
Educators38%55%
Tech workers29%44%
Retail workers35%49%
Finance workers31%39%

Psychologist Dr. Marcus Webb argues that the COVID-19 pandemic disproportionately increased stress among workers in public-facing occupations compared to those who could transition to remote work. He further claims that healthcare workers experienced the most dramatic increase in reported stress of any occupational group studied.
Which choice best uses data from the table to support Dr. Webb's claim?

choices: Healthcare workers reported the highest stress levels in both 2019 and 2022, suggesting that their work has always been more stressful than other occupations.

Healthcare workers saw a 27 percentage point increase in reported stress — the largest of any group — while finance workers, who could more easily work remotely, saw only an 8 percentage point increase.
All five occupational groups showed increased stress levels between 2019 and 2022, indicating that the pandemic affected workers across all sectors.
Tech workers, despite being able to work remotely, still experienced a 15 percentage point increase in stress, which challenges Dr. Webb's claim.
questions.explanation:
For Passage + Table questions, the correct answer must use specific data to support both parts of the claim. Dr. Webb claims (1) public-facing workers were disproportionately affected compared to remote workers, and (2) healthcare workers had the most dramatic increase of any group.
A) States that healthcare workers had the highest stress levels in both years, but this addresses absolute levels, not the size of the increase. It also does not address the comparison with remote workers. Eliminate A.
B) Healthcare workers increased by 27 percentage points (41% to 68%) — the largest increase in the table — while finance workers increased by only 8 points (31% to 39%). This directly supports both parts of Dr. Webb’s claim using specific data. Keep B.
C) States that all groups showed increased stress, which is too vague and does not address the disproportionate impact on public-facing workers or the healthcare finding. Eliminate C.
D) Frames tech workers’ increase as a challenge to Dr. Webb’s claim rather than supporting it. The goal is to support, not undermine. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

questions.subject: verbal

stemSegments.text: The following text is from Frederick Douglass’s Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave, published in 1845.

I have often been utterly astonished, since I came to the north, to find persons who could speak of the singing, among slaves, as evidence of their contentment and happiness. It is impossible to <u>conceive of</u> a greater mistake. Slaves sing most when they are most unhappy. The songs of the slave represent the sorrows of his heart; and he is relieved by them, only as an aching heart is relieved by its tears.
As used in the text, “conceive of” most nearly means

choices: imagine
approve of
design
tolerate
questions.explanation:
For Vocabulary in Context questions at level 3, the tone and content of the passage can make emotionally loaded choices seem plausible. Here, Douglass says “It is impossible to conceive of a greater mistake” — the blank must be a cognitive act (something the mind does), not a moral or emotional one.
A) “Imagine” means to form a mental picture or concept. “Conceive of” in this context means to think of or imagine — “it is impossible to imagine a greater mistake.” This fits precisely. Keep A.
B) “Approve of” is tempting because Douglass is clearly disapproving of those who misread slave singing as happiness. However, the phrase is about cognitive impossibility, not moral disapproval — he is saying no one could even think of a larger error, not that he won’t endorse it. Eliminate B.
C) “Design” means to plan or create. This does not fit the context — Douglass is not discussing creating a mistake. Eliminate C.
D) “Tolerate” is also tempting given Douglass’s tone of moral outrage, but again, the phrase is cognitive rather than moral. He is not saying the mistake is intolerable — he is saying it is impossible to even think of a worse one. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is A.

questions.subject: verbal

stemSegments.text: Historian Dr. James Okafor argues that the Harlem Renaissance of the 1920s was not primarily a literary movement, as it is most commonly characterized, but rather a visual arts movement whose impact on American culture has been systematically undervalued by scholars who prioritize written texts. He contends that the painters, sculptors, and photographers of the period reached broader audiences and had more immediate cultural influence than the writers who have received the bulk of scholarly attention.
Which finding, if true, would most directly undermine Dr. Okafor’s claim?

choices: Several Harlem Renaissance writers, including Langston Hughes, gained significant international recognition during the 1920s.
Surveys of Americans conducted in the 1920s show that written works of the Harlem Renaissance were more widely read and discussed than visual artworks of the same period were viewed.
Scholars have published significantly more books about Harlem Renaissance literature than about its visual arts.
Many Harlem Renaissance visual artists also worked as illustrators for literary magazines, blurring the boundary between visual and written culture.
questions.explanation:
For Weakening a Claim questions, identify the claim precisely. Dr. Okafor claims that visual arts reached broader audiences and had more immediate cultural influence than the writers. To undermine this, the correct answer must show that literary works actually had broader reach or more influence.
A) International recognition for Harlem Renaissance writers is consistent with them having broad influence, but it does not directly compare their reach to that of visual artists. Eliminate A.
B) If written works were more widely read and discussed than visual artworks were viewed, this directly contradicts Okafor’s claim that visual arts reached broader audiences. Keep B.
C) Scholars publishing more about literature actually supports Okafor’s claim that scholars have prioritized written texts over visual arts. Eliminate C.
D) Visual artists working as literary illustrators is an interesting connection, but it does not address whether visual or written works had broader audiences or more cultural influence. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

questions.subject: verbal
questions.stem:
The philosopher Karl Popper held that scientific theories can never be proven true — they can only be falsified. No amount of confirming evidence, he argued, is sufficient to establish a theory as certainly correct, because a single counterexample is enough to disprove it. This asymmetry, Popper claimed, is what distinguishes science from other forms of inquiry: genuine scientific theories make bold predictions that could in principle be shown wrong. Theories constructed so as to accommodate any possible evidence — that can explain everything equally well — are, by Popper’s account, not scientific at all.

Which choice best states the main idea of the text?
choices: Popper argued that scientific theories are more reliable than other forms of knowledge because they can be confirmed through repeated experimentation.
According to Popper, what makes a theory scientific is not that it can be proven true, but that it makes predictions specific enough to be potentially disproven.
Popper’s philosophy of science demonstrated that all existing scientific theories will eventually be falsified by new evidence.
Popper believed that counterexamples are more valuable to science than confirming evidence because they generate new research questions.
questions.explanation:
For Main Idea questions at level 3, the correct answer must capture the central argument precisely. Before looking at choices, summarize: Popper argued that theories cannot be proven true, only falsified, and that the ability to be falsified is what makes a theory scientific.
A) Directly contradicts Popper’s view — he argued that no amount of confirming evidence proves a theory true. The passage explicitly states this. Eliminate A.
B) Captures the asymmetry at the heart of Popper’s argument: theories can’t be proven true, but they can be falsified, and the capacity to be falsified is the criterion for being scientific. Keep B.
C) Overstates Popper’s position — he argued that theories are falsifiable in principle, not that all theories will inevitably be falsified. Eliminate C.
D) Too narrow — Popper’s point about counterexamples is that they can disprove theories, not specifically that they generate new research questions. The passage does not mention research questions. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

questions.stem:
Though celebrated for her meticulous scholarship, the historian's critics argued that her apparent objectivity was itself a pose — that her selection of sources and framing of events reflected commitments far from _______, however neutral her prose style appeared on the surface.

Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?
choices: disinterested
impartial
detached
rigorous
questions.explanation:
For Vocabulary in Context questions at level 3, the blank must capture a precise quality the critics say the historian lacks. The critics argue her “apparent objectivity was itself a pose” — her source selection and framing revealed underlying commitments despite a neutral prose surface. The blank must mean something like genuinely neutral or free from bias.
A) “Impartial” means treating all sides equally — close, but the key distinction is that “disinterested” specifically means having no personal stake or vested interest in the outcome, which is the quality critics say her source selection undermines. Eliminate A.
B) “Disinterested” means free from personal interest or bias — precisely what the critics argue she is not, since her framing reveals underlying commitments. This is also a classic vocabulary trap: students often confuse “disinterested” (unbiased) with “uninterested” (indifferent). Keep B.
C) “Detached” means emotionally removed — the critics are questioning her intellectual bias, not her emotional involvement. Eliminate C.
D) “Rigorous” means thorough and demanding — her scholarship is described as meticulous, so calling it “far from rigorous” would contradict the passage. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

questions.stem:
After years of combative press conferences and pointed public criticism of his rivals, the senator surprised observers with a remarkably _______ address — one that extended olive branches to former opponents, acknowledged areas of common ground, and deliberately avoided the sharp-edged rhetoric that had defined his earlier career.

Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?
choices: subdued
measured
diplomatic
conciliatory
questions.explanation:
For Vocabulary in Context questions at level 3, the direction change is key. After “years of combative” behavior, the senator surprised with the opposite. The blank describes a speech that “extended olive branches,” “acknowledged common ground,” and avoided sharp rhetoric. The word must capture the specific quality of seeking reconciliation.
A) “Subdued” means quiet or restrained in tone — while the address was less combative, the key feature is the active reaching out to opponents, which “subdued” doesn’t capture. Eliminate A.
B) “Conciliatory” means intended to make peace and reduce hostility — precisely what extending olive branches, acknowledging common ground, and avoiding sharp rhetoric accomplishes. Keep B.
C) “Measured” means careful and deliberate — describes manner of speech, not the intent to reconcile. Eliminate C.
D) “Diplomatic” means tactful — close, but “conciliatory” is more precise because it specifically means aimed at reconciliation with former opponents, not just general tactfulness. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

stemSegments.text: The following text is from Zora Neale Hurston's Dust Tracks on a Road, published in 1942.

I was born in a Negro town. I do not mean by that the black back-side of an average town. Eatonville, Florida is, and was at the time of my birth, a pure Negro town — charter, mayor, council, and all. It was not the first Negro town in the United States, but it was the first to be incorporated, the first to assume a governing body, and as such it stands as a unique element in the history of the United States.
According to the text, what does Hurston emphasize about Eatonville that distinguishes it from other Black communities?

choices: It was the first Black town to establish a formal governing structure through legal incorporation.
It was the first town in the United States founded and populated entirely by Black residents.
It was larger and more politically active than other Black communities of its era.
It was founded specifically to serve as a model for Black self-governance across the South.
questions.explanation:
For According to the Text questions, the correct answer must be directly stated. Hurston says Eatonville “was not the first Negro town in the United States, but it was the first to be incorporated, the first to assume a governing body.”
A) Hurston explicitly says it was NOT the first Negro town — this contradicts the passage. Eliminate A.
B) Directly stated: first to be incorporated and assume a governing body — formal legal self-governance. Keep B.
C) Size and political activity level are not mentioned. Eliminate C.
D) Hurston does not describe Eatonville as a model or as founded for a specific purpose. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

stemSegments.text: The following text is from Rachel Carson's The Sea Around Us, published in 1951.

Beginnings are apt to be shadowy, and so it is with the beginnings of that great mother of life, the sea. Many people have debated how and when the ocean came to be. The poets and philosophers who first turned their minds to this question imagined that a watery chaos preceded the creation of the world. Science has not altered that conception greatly; it merely has substituted new images. The ocean is ancient beyond all our concepts of time. It is older than the mountains, older than the continents, older than life itself on this earth.
According to the text, how does Carson characterize the relationship between scientific and earlier accounts of the ocean's origins?

choices: Science has conclusively disproved the philosophical and poetic accounts that preceded it.
Science has largely confirmed that early accounts were correct about the ocean's extraordinary age.
Science has replaced vague poetic imagery with precise explanations, fundamentally changing our understanding.
Science has not dramatically changed the essential conception, only the specific images used to express it.
questions.explanation:
For According to the Text questions, the correct answer must match what Carson directly states. She writes: “Science has not altered that conception greatly; it merely has substituted new images.”
A) Carson says science has NOT altered the conception greatly — the opposite of disproving it. Eliminate A.
B) Carson doesn’t say science confirmed correctness about age specifically — she says the conception wasn’t greatly altered. Eliminate B.
C) Carson says science “merely substituted new images” — implying the change was minimal, not fundamental. Eliminate C.
D) Directly matches: “not altered that conception greatly” and “merely substituted new images.” Keep D.


The correct answer is D.

questions.stem:
When the Hubble Space Telescope was launched in 1990, scientists discovered that its primary mirror had been ground to the wrong shape — a flaw of just 2.2 micrometers that nonetheless caused every image it produced to be blurry. NASA engineers developed corrective optics, essentially a set of small mirrors designed to compensate for the aberration, and astronauts installed them during a 1993 servicing mission. The repair was considered an engineering triumph, and Hubble subsequently produced some of the most scientifically significant astronomical images ever captured.

According to the text, what caused the initial problem with the Hubble Space Telescope?
choices: The telescope was launched before engineers had finished calibrating its optical systems.
Damage sustained during launch affected the telescope's ability to focus correctly.
A tiny error in the shape of the primary mirror caused all of its images to be blurry.
The telescope's corrective optics failed to function as designed once it reached orbit.
questions.explanation:
For According to the Text questions, the correct answer is directly stated. The passage says the primary mirror “had been ground to the wrong shape — a flaw of just 2.2 micrometers that nonetheless caused every image it produced to be blurry.”
A) Calibration is not mentioned — the problem was the mirror’s shape. Eliminate A.
B) Directly matches: a tiny error in the mirror’s shape caused blurry images. Keep B.
C) Launch damage is not mentioned — the flaw was in how the mirror was ground before launch. Eliminate C.
D) The corrective optics were the solution, not the problem. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

questions.stem:
The Voynich manuscript, a handwritten illustrated codex believed to date from the early fifteenth century, has resisted all attempts at decipherment since its rediscovery by antiquarian Wilfrid Voynich in 1912. The manuscript is written in an unknown script and an unknown language — or possibly in a cipher — and its illustrations depict plants, astronomical diagrams, and human figures engaged in unexplained activities, none of which have been definitively identified. Some scholars argue it is an elaborate hoax containing no meaningful content; others contend it encodes genuine information in a system no one has yet cracked. The manuscript is held at Yale University's Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

According to the text, what do scholars disagree about regarding the Voynich manuscript?
choices: Whether the manuscript was genuinely written in the fifteenth century or is a modern forgery.
Whether the illustrations depict real plants and astronomical phenomena or invented ones.
Whether the manuscript contains meaningful encoded information or is simply a sophisticated hoax.
Whether Wilfrid Voynich discovered the manuscript legitimately or fabricated it himself.
questions.explanation:
For According to the Text questions, the correct answer is directly stated. The passage says: “Some scholars argue it is an elaborate hoax containing no meaningful content; others contend it encodes genuine information in a system no one has yet cracked.”
A) The passage does not mention disagreement about the manuscript’s dating or whether it is a modern forgery. Eliminate A.
B) Disagreement about whether the illustrations are real or invented is not described. Eliminate B.
C) Directly matches the passage: hoax (no meaningful content) vs. encoded genuine information. Keep C.
D) Voynich’s role in fabricating the manuscript is not discussed. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is C.

questions.stem:
Cognitive scientist Dr. Elena Vasquez argues that bilingual children develop stronger executive function than their monolingual peers — specifically, greater ability to suppress irrelevant information and switch attention between tasks — because the constant management of two language systems exercises the same cognitive control mechanisms that underlie these skills.

Which finding, if true, would most directly support Dr. Vasquez's claim?
choices: Bilingual children score higher than monolingual children on standardized tests of vocabulary knowledge in their dominant language.
Children who learn a second language before age five show greater executive function advantages than those who learn a second language after age ten.
Adults who are fluent in three or more languages show greater executive function advantages than those who are fluent in two languages.

In a controlled experiment, bilingual children outperformed monolingual children on tasks requiring them to ignore a distracting stimulus and shift focus between competing rules, but not on tasks measuring memory capacity.
questions.explanation:
For Supporting a Claim questions, identify the precise claim. Dr. Vasquez argues bilingualism specifically improves executive function — the ability to suppress irrelevant information and switch attention — because managing two languages exercises those exact mechanisms.
A) Vocabulary scores address language proficiency, not executive function. Eliminate A.
B) Bilingual children outperforming on tasks requiring suppression of distraction and attention-switching — precisely the executive function skills Dr. Vasquez identifies — directly supports her claim. The qualifier “but not on memory capacity” also makes the finding more credible and specific. Keep B.
C) The age of acquisition addresses timing, not whether bilingualism improves executive function at all. Eliminate C.
D) Trilingual vs bilingual comparison extends beyond Dr. Vasquez’s specific claim about bilingual vs monolingual. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

questions.stem:
Economist Dr. Rashid Okafor argues that moderate increases in the minimum wage — defined as increases of 10 to 15 percent — do not cause significant reductions in employment in low-wage sectors, and that concerns about job losses have been consistently overstated by critics of minimum wage legislation. He bases this claim primarily on studies comparing employment levels in counties on opposite sides of state borders where minimum wages differ.

Which finding, if true, would most directly undermine Dr. Okafor's claim?
choices: A meta-analysis of 47 minimum wage studies found that the average employment effect across all studies was a reduction of fewer than 0.5% of jobs in affected sectors.

Counties used in border-comparison studies often differ significantly in industry composition, unionization rates, and local economic conditions in ways that make direct employment comparisons unreliable.
Several countries that implemented large minimum wage increases of over 30% experienced significant employment reductions in their restaurant and retail sectors.
Economists who specialize in labor markets are more likely than general economists to find negative employment effects from minimum wage increases.
questions.explanation:
For Weakening a Claim questions, identify the most precise element to target. Dr. Okafor bases his claim about moderate minimum wage increases on border-comparison studies. To undermine his claim, the correct answer must challenge either the conclusion or the evidentiary basis.
A) A 0.5% average employment reduction across studies is very small — this actually supports Dr. Okafor’s claim that job losses are overstated. Eliminate A.
B) If the border-comparison counties differ in ways that make employment comparisons unreliable, this directly undermines the evidentiary basis of Dr. Okafor’s claim — his primary evidence becomes suspect. Keep B.
C) Large increases (30%+) are outside the moderate range (10–15%) Dr. Okafor studied — this doesn’t directly undermine his specific claim. Eliminate C.
D) Economists’ specialization affecting findings is a general observation about research bias, not a direct challenge to Dr. Okafor’s evidence or conclusion. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.

stemSegments.text: While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

• Fingerprint evidence has been used in criminal courts since the early twentieth century and was long considered among the most reliable forms of forensic identification.
• In 2009, the National Academy of Sciences released a landmark report concluding that fingerprint analysis lacks a standardized methodology and that examiners’ conclusions are often subjective.
• The report found no peer-reviewed scientific studies establishing the rate at which fingerprint examiners make errors.
• Several wrongful convictions have been linked in part to fingerprint misidentification, including the 2004 case of Brandon Mayfield, who was falsely identified by FBI examiners in connection with the Madrid train bombings.
• Some courts have begun requiring fingerprint examiners to disclose their error rates and the methodology used to reach conclusions.

The student wants to explain why courts have begun scrutinizing fingerprint evidence more carefully in recent years. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices: Fingerprint evidence has been used in criminal courts since the early twentieth century, when it was considered one of the most reliable forms of forensic identification available.
Several wrongful convictions, including the false identification of Brandon Mayfield in 2004, have demonstrated that fingerprint misidentification can have serious consequences for defendants.

A 2009 National Academy of Sciences report found that fingerprint analysis lacks standardized methodology, that examiners’ conclusions are often subjective, and that no studies had established examiner error rates — findings that directly challenged the scientific foundation of fingerprint evidence.
Some courts now require fingerprint examiners to disclose their error rates and explain the methodology used to reach their conclusions before presenting evidence.
questions.explanation:
For Rhetorical Synthesis questions at level 3, the goal is to explain why courts have begun scrutinizing fingerprint evidence more carefully. The correct answer must identify what prompted this increased scrutiny.
A) Describes the historical status of fingerprint evidence — before courts began scrutinizing it more carefully, not the reason for the change. Eliminate A.
B) Wrongful convictions demonstrate consequences but don’t explain the mechanism by which scientific credibility was challenged — they are an effect, not the primary cause of increased scrutiny. Eliminate B.
C) The 2009 NAS report is the direct cause — it challenged the scientific foundation of fingerprint evidence by identifying lack of standardization, subjectivity, and unknown error rates, giving courts a formal basis for increased scrutiny. Keep C.
D) Describes a current practice — the result of increased scrutiny, not the explanation for why it began. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is C.

stemSegments.text: While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

• Diel vertical migration is the daily movement of marine organisms from deep water to the surface at night and back again before dawn.
• It is considered the largest daily animal migration on Earth by biomass.
• Some species travel distances of 200 to 1,000 meters vertically within a single 24-hour cycle.
• Organisms migrate upward at night to feed on surface plankton in darkness, reducing their own predation risk, then descend to colder, darker water during the day.
• Estimates suggest that organisms performing diel vertical migration collectively transport tens of millions of tons of carbon from surface waters to the deep ocean each year, a process known as the biological pump.

The student wants to convey the scale of diel vertical migration using the most specific and striking evidence available in the notes. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices: Diel vertical migration is considered the largest daily animal migration on Earth by biomass, with some species traveling up to 1,000 meters vertically within a single day.
Diel vertical migration involves marine organisms traveling between deep water and the ocean surface each night to feed on plankton while avoiding predators.
Through diel vertical migration, marine organisms collectively transport tens of millions of tons of carbon from the surface to the deep ocean annually, a process known as the biological pump.
Diel vertical migration occurs daily across the world’s oceans, making it a phenomenon of global ecological significance that affects both surface and deep-water ecosystems.
questions.explanation:
For Rhetorical Synthesis questions at level 3, the goal is to convey scale using the most specific and striking evidence. The correct answer must combine quantitative specificity with the most impressive scale indicators.
A) Describes the mechanism (feeding at night, avoiding predators) — relevant but conveys behavior, not scale. Eliminate A.
B) Combines the superlative claim (largest daily animal migration on Earth by biomass) with the most striking specific measurement (up to 1,000 meters per day) — the most effective combination for conveying scale. Keep B.
C) The carbon transport statistic (tens of millions of tons) is impressive and specific, but it describes an indirect effect of the migration rather than the scale of the migration itself. Eliminate C.
D) States global significance vaguely without any specific quantitative evidence. Eliminate D.


The correct answer is B.
